_meta._about: Content for the universal '?' info chips (consumed by app/explain.js → Explain.chip/attach). Each entry is keyed by a metric_id. The integrator maps each key to the explain.js model shape: title_he→title, what_he→gloss/summary lead, how_he→formula/method, data_he→data rows + drawer text, source→sources[], caveat_he→assumptions/estimate note. 'kind' is measured | modeled | ephemeris | hybrid | static — drives the 'estimate' badge (everything except pure 'measured'/'ephemeris' should show it).
_meta._honesty: Labels follow CLAUDE.md: 'based on real measurements' for Open-Meteo weather; 'computed from the house geometry' for geometry-derived; ephemeris for sun/moon/planets is near-exact; NEVER implies a physical soil/temperature sensor.

goOutScore.what_he: How worthwhile it is to go out stargazing tonight from your yard, 0–100.
goOutScore.how_he: score = (1−cloud)·60 + (1−moon illumination)·28 + transparency·12. Cloud is the dominant 'killer'. transparency = 1 − humidity penalty − dust penalty (humidity above ~40% and dust above ~25µg/m³ dim the stars). No light-pollution penalty: your sky is Bortle 3 (dark rural sky) — so a clear, dry, moonless night here reaches 'excellent', while the same night over a city would only be 'okay'.
data_he: Tonight's cloud: Weather.tonightCloud — 21:00–23:00 average from the Open-Meteo forecast (measured/forecast)
Dust: Weather.air.pm10 or the state.dust estimate (measured when air-quality is available)

source.name: Open-Meteo + internal model (derive.js goOutScore); Bortle: site.json

goOutScore.caveat_he: Cloud and dust are forecast — they can change by nightfall. Transparency is an estimate from humidity/dust, not an optical visibility measurement. The score is a guidance tool, not a promise.

transparency.what_he: How 'clean' the sky is from the humidity and dust that dim stars, 0–1.
transparency.how_he: transparency = max(0, 1 − humidity_penalty − dust_penalty). humidity_penalty = clamp((RH−40)/55)·0.6, dust_penalty = clamp((dust−25)/120)·0.7. A dry 'good' default when there's no data — we don't invent a penalty.
transparency.caveat_he: An empirical parameterization of light attenuation from humidity and dust — not an actual visibility measurement. Estimate.

microclimate_temp.what_he: The Larkmont station's temperature 'brought down' to your plot — a small local offset from the town reading.
microclimate_temp.how_he: temp_house = temp_town + Δ. Δ = lapse (elevation correction, ~0 here because both the town and the house sit on the ridge) + micro. By day: a sheltered/sunlit corner is slightly warmer; a shaded corner is cooler. On a clear, calm night: an open surface (high SVF) radiates to the sky and cools; a raised terrace is warmer because cold air drains downward.
data_he: SVF + wind exposure of the yard cell: house geometry (computed)
microclimate_temp.caveat_he: The Δ is derived from a parameterization of radiative cooling and cold-air drainage based on the cell's exposure and visible sky — not a CFD simulation and not a sensor. Estimate (usually ±1–2°C).

airDelta.title_he: The corner's temperature offset from the town
airDelta.what_he: By how many degrees a specific corner of the yard is warmer/colder than the town's 'official' reading.
airDelta.how_he: Δ = lapse + micro. lapse = −(house_elevation − 300)·0.0065 (temperature lapse rate with elevation). micro by day = (1−exposure)·1.0 + (sunlit? +0.6 : −1.2); micro by night = radiative cooling −(0.4+1.6·SVF)·clarity factor·calm factor + drainage (terrace +1.2).
data_he: SVF + wind exposure: computed from house geometry (constant per cell)
Sun/shade state of the cell: Astro + shadowMask (computed)
airDelta.caveat_he: The cooling and drainage coefficients are physically calibrated but were not measured on the plot. Estimate.

surfaceTemp.what_he: How hot the surface itself is (soil/wall/gravel) — not the air temperature, but the skin exposed to the sun.
surfaceTemp.how_he: Steady-state energy balance, closed-form solution: T = (α·S + h_c·T_air + h_rs·T_sky + h_rg·T_ground + h_cd·T_deep) / (h_c+h_rs+h_rg+h_cd). S = absorbed radiation (POA: direct+diffuse+reflected), h_c = convection (McAdams 5.7+3.8·V), h_rs/h_rg = long-wave black-body radiation to the sky/ground (T_sky from Brunt emissivity), h_cd = conduction to depth. A small single-pole lag correction based on the thermal mass.
data_he: GHI radiation: Weather.envAt('rad') (measured) or a clear-sky model
α (absorption), SVF, exposure, surface normal: geometry + material (computed)
surfaceTemp.caveat_he: A physical estimate (model·estimate), not a sensor and not CFD/ENVI-met. A real leaf cools through transpiration (which we ignore), so the gravel reading runs hotter than the foliage.

indoorTemp.title_he: How hot it is inside the house (no air conditioning)
indoorTemp.what_he: An estimate of the air temperature inside the heavy stone house, without air conditioning — a much smaller swing than outside, peaking a few hours later.
indoorTemp.how_he: An exponential moving average (EMA, time constant ~10 h) of the 'reconstructed' outdoor-temperature curve: the seasonal daily shape (min ~05:30, max ~15:00) re-anchored to the live reading, so the thermal mass dampens and delays it. A slight anchoring (15%) to the measured soil temperature is added, plus a small bump for sun-drenched days.
indoorTemp.caveat_he: There's no sensor in the house — this is a reconstruction from a typical daily shape + anchoring to outside and to the soil. Estimate; the peak lags ~10 h and at a small amplitude.

frostRisk.what_he: Whether sensitive tissue in the open yard could drop below 0°C on a clear, calm night — the worst-case radiative frost scenario.
frostRisk.how_he: We compute the surface temperature of a horizontal leaf at dawn using the same energy balance (no sun, only Brunt cooling to the sky). We cross-check it against 0°C and against the dew point. score = 0.55·frost proximity + 0.30·radiative-cooling tendency (clarity·calm·SVF) + 0.15·dryness.
data_he: SVF + exposure of the yard cell: geometry (computed)
frostRisk.caveat_he: A physical worst-case forecast (clear, calm night), not a promise. Larkmont gets radiative frost nearly year-round. Estimate.

dewPoint.what_he: The temperature at which the air becomes saturated and dew/fog begins to condense.
dewPoint.how_he: Magnus formula from air temperature and humidity: γ = ln(RH/100) + 17.62·T/(243.12+T); Td = 243.12·γ/(17.62−γ).
dewPoint.caveat_he: A standard calculation from real measurements; accuracy depends on the accuracy of the humidity reading.

feelsLike.what_he: How the temperature actually feels given humidity and wind (Steadman).
feelsLike.how_he: AT = T + 0.33·e − 0.70·ws − 4.00, where e = (RH/100)·6.105·exp(17.27·T/(237.7+T)) is the vapor pressure (hPa) and ws is the wind speed (m/s).
feelsLike.caveat_he: A standard AT formula from real measurements. In the dry region the wind usually lowers the perceived temperature.

dli.what_he: The amount of photosynthetically active photons (PAR) a yard cell receives per day, mol·m⁻²·d⁻¹ — what determines whether a plant gets enough light.
dli.how_he: DLI = Σ PPFD·Δt / 1e6, where PPFD ≈ 2.02 µmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ per W/m² of the radiation hitting the cell (incidentSolar: direct·shadowMask + diffuse·SVF + reflected). In seasonal mode: stepping over a representative day (the 15th of the month) at 10 min. In Living-Record: over that day's actual measured radiation.
data_he: GHI radiation: measured (Open-Meteo) in the live record, or a clear-sky model in seasonal mode
dli.caveat_he: The broadband→PAR conversion (2.02) is a typical literature coefficient. The radiation in the live record is measured; the geometry is computed. In seasonal mode — an estimate from climatology.

sunHours.what_he: How many hours a day the sun directly hits this cell — not just 'daytime', but actual sunbeams after the ridge, the house and the neighbors.
sunHours.how_he: We sum the minutes when the sun is above the ridge's true horizon and the beam from the cell is clear of all blockers (house, shed, neighbors), and divide by 60. shadowMask=1 only when the sun 'sees' the cell.
data_he: True ridge horizon: data/horizon.json (measured from the terrain)
Blockers: geometry boxes of the house/shed/neighbors (computed)
sunHours.caveat_he: The blockers' geometry is coarse (boxes), and the low walls are excluded from the validated baseline (zone_sun_hours.json). A geometric estimate.

frostTdawn.what_he: The temperature a sensitive leaf in this cell is expected to see at dawn on a representative frost night — determines where to plant frost-sensitive species.
frostTdawn.how_he: frostTdawn = radiative cold peak (Tlow_winter−3) + drainage (raised cell +3.2 / low cell −1.8·SVF, collects a cold pool) + radiative dip (−2.4·SVF). This explains why a frost-sensitive avocado is placed precisely up on the terrace.
frostTdawn.caveat_he: Cold-air drainage and the radiative dip are parameterized, not simulated. Estimate — a relative indication between corners, not an absolute number.

ETc.what_he: How much water the plot/plant evaporates per day (evaporation+transpiration), mm/day — the basis for an irrigation recommendation.
ETc.how_he: ETc = ET0 · (0.3 + 0.7·solar-capture fraction), where the fraction = the radiation the cell captures divided by the open sky. ET0 (open-field reference) is measured from Open-Meteo in the live record, or climatological in seasonal mode. That is, a shaded cell evaporates less than an exposed one.
data_he: ET0: Open-Meteo et0_fao (measured) or climatological in seasonal mode
ETc.caveat_he: ET0 is measured; the shading follows the geometry. There's no full plant-specific Kc here — a solar-capture approximation. Estimate.

chillHours.what_he: How many winter hours the temperature is in the 0–7.2°C range — deciduous fruit trees (apple, cherry) require a minimum in order to bloom.
chillHours.how_he: We count the hours in the chill range from the seasonal daily curve and multiply by the number of winter days (~90). In the live record: hours with a measured air temperature in 0–7°C.
data_he: In the live record: measured temperature (Open-Meteo)
chillHours.caveat_he: The seasonal estimate is climatological (an estimate). The chill count in the live record is based on real measurements.

gdd.what_he: Heat accumulation above a 10°C base that drives growth and fruit ripening — the plant's 'growth clock'.
gdd.how_he: GDD_daily = max(0, min(30, T_mean) − 10), accumulated over the season. Base 10°C, cap 30°C. In seasonal mode from climatology; in the live record from each day's measured air temperature.
data_he: Daily mean T: measured (Open-Meteo) in the live record, climatological in seasonal mode
gdd.caveat_he: Generic base/cap (not calibrated to a specific species). The seasonal value is an estimate; the live record is from real measurements.

plantScore.what_he: How well a particular plant fits a specific corner/season, 0–100 — it weighs light, heat, frost, winter chill, water and wind.
plantScore.how_he: score = 100·(w_dli·fit(DLI) + w_sun·fit(sun) + w_chill·fit(chill) + w_water·fit(water)) − 100·(w_heat·heat penalty + w_frost·frost penalty + w_wind·wind penalty). fit()=1 within the range, dropping to 0 outside it. For a frost-sensitive plant the frost weight is increased and the wind penalty is softened (because it's planted in a sheltered spot).
data_he: Plant requirements: resident_plants.json / plant_candidates.json (dli_min/max, t_max_tol, frost_hardy_c, chill_hours_req…)
Cell profile: cellProfile (DLI, sun, frostTdawn, foliage heat — computed)
plantScore.caveat_he: The weights are heuristic and the profile is a model. An agronomic guidance tool, not a guarantee. Estimate.

roofSolar_annual_kwh.what_he: How many kWh a year your actual house roof could generate from a PV system — a hyper-local physical estimate, not Google Solar (which has no 3D mesh for Larkmont).
roofSolar_annual_kwh.how_he: annual_kWh = POA_calibrated · usable area · module efficiency · performance ratio (PR). POA is accumulated by stepping over the 15th of each month through the same incidentSolar engine that casts sunbeams against the true ridge horizon, the parapet and the neighbors. A 1.30 calibration factor lifts the GHI to the 'clear, dry sky' measured in the TMY.
data_he: Roof area ~41.7 m² (two steps, from building.js), usable ~65%
roofSolar_annual_kwh.caveat_he: Estimate (±10–15%). Usable area is a geometric approximation (not a real packing solution). The 1.30 calibration factor is documented. Model·estimate — not a measurement.

roofSolar_horizon_loss.what_he: What percentage of the solar generation is 'eaten' by the ridge, the parapet and the neighbors blocking the sun — the unique advantage of this hyper-local computation.
roofSolar_horizon_loss.how_he: horizon_loss = 100·(1 − POA_with_shading / POA_flat_horizon). We run the same model twice: once with ray casting against the true geometry, and once against an ideal flat horizon (mask=1), and the difference is the loss.
roofSolar_horizon_loss.caveat_he: A ratio between two model runs — therefore unaffected by the calibration factor. A geometric estimate.

solar_hot_water_fraction.what_he: What fraction of your hot-water consumption the solar water-heater supplies each month — when electric backup is needed.
solar_hot_water_fraction.how_he: fraction = min(1, energy supplied / demand). Supplied = monthly GHI · collector area (2.5 m²) · system efficiency; demand from 120 L/day of hot water. December is the only month that drops below 1 (≈0.90) → backup needed.
data_he: Monthly climatological GHI, 2.5 m² collector, 150 L tank, 120 L/day demand
solar_hot_water_fraction.caveat_he: A monthly estimate from climatology and a fixed demand assumption — not a meter reading. Actual consumption varies.

degree_days.what_he: How much the house 'demands' heating (HDD) or cooling (CDD) over the year — an estimate of the climatic load.
degree_days.how_he: HDD = Σ max(0, 18°C − T_daily), CDD = Σ max(0, T_daily − 18°C), over the year. Annual: HDD ~1132, CDD ~766 — a dry plateau region: a winter that demands heating and a summer that demands cooling.
degree_days.caveat_he: Based on monthly normals, not an actual annual measurement. Estimate.

night_flush.what_he: The window of hours for opening windows at night to cool the house with cool night air instead of an air conditioner.
night_flush.how_he: open_hours = open_end − open_start, the hours when the outdoor temperature is below the target indoor temperature. In the cool months (Oct–Apr) it isn't needed at all. May–Sep: a window of 11–17 h from evening to morning.
night_flush.caveat_he: A monthly-average model; it assumes outdoor temp < indoor at night. Estimate — adjust to the specific night.

dust_aqi.what_he: Particle concentration (PM2.5/PM10), an air-quality index, and the actual suspended dust over the plot.
dust_aqi.how_he: Live readings from Open-Meteo Air-Quality (no key). The dust intensity in the 3D scene = clamp(dust/250) or derived from PM10, so the badge and the haze stay in sync.
data_he: pm2_5, pm10, european_aqi, dust, uv: Weather.air (measured, Open-Meteo Air-Quality)
dust_aqi.caveat_he: Based on regional model measurements (CAMS) — not a yard sensor. When there's no data, the scene's dust intensity is derived from wind/humidity (an approximation).

uv_index.what_he: The ultraviolet radiation intensity right now — up on the high plateau it is extreme.
uv_index.how_he: A live reading from Open-Meteo (uv_index and uv_index_clear_sky). There's no internal formula here — a directly measured/forecast value.
uv_index.caveat_he: A regional model/measurement value. At ~300 m elevation and in the dry region the actual UV is among the highest measured.

accumPrecip.what_he: How much rain actually fell over the plot in the last 30 days, mm.
accumPrecip.how_he: Summing the daily precipitation_sum from Open-Meteo over the last 30 days.
accumPrecip.caveat_he: Based on regional real measurements. In the dry region the total is usually very low — some days are zero.

pollen.what_he: Which pollens are active in the current season in the region — relevant to allergies.
pollen.how_he: A seasonal table for the region (not a live reading) — cross-referencing the current month against the bloom seasons of the common species.
pollen.caveat_he: A general seasonal table for the region — not a live pollen count and not specific to Larkmont. An indication.

noonAlt.what_he: How high the sun reaches at the day's peak — the clearest read of the season (summer solstice ~79.4°, winter ~32.6°).
noonAlt.how_he: NOAA sun model: solar declination via the ecliptic longitude L that sweeps ±23.44° over the year. We scan the day at 2 min and take the maximum elevation. Validated against known noon elevations at this latitude.
noonAlt.caveat_he: Ephemeris — accurate to a fraction of a degree. Not affected by weather.

twilightTimes.what_he: When the sun crosses the elevation bands: golden hour +6°, civil twilight −6°, nautical −12°, astronomical −18° (full darkness).
twilightTimes.how_he: We scan the day at 2 min with the sun model and mark the moments when the elevation crosses each threshold — rising (morning) and falling (evening).
twilightTimes.caveat_he: Ephemeris accurate to the minute. The actual darkness for observing also depends on cloud and the moon.

sunEvents.what_he: When the sun actually rises over your true ridge and sets behind it — different from a flat-horizon sunrise.
sunEvents.how_he: We scan the day and compare two crossings: against the true horizon (data/horizon.json) and against a flat horizon (0°). The eastern/western ridge delays/advances the sunrise/sunset relative to the generic calculation.
data_he: True ridge horizon: data/horizon.json (measured from the terrain)
sunEvents.caveat_he: The sun's path is ephemeris; the horizon is measured from terrain data (DEM) — not guaranteed to the minute when there's haze.

nextMeteor.what_he: The upcoming meteor shower visible from your dark sky, with its peak and time.
nextMeteor.how_he: A fixed 2026 peak schedule (Perseids, Geminids…) with ZHR (meteors/hour at peak); we return the first one that hasn't yet passed and the number of days until it.
data_he: Hard-coded 2026 peak schedule (METEORS) with date and expected ZHR
nextMeteor.caveat_he: The peak dates are known in advance; the ZHR is theoretical (perfect sky, radiant at zenith) — the actual count is usually lower and depends on cloud and the moon.

nextDarkNight.what_he: The upcoming night with the least moonlight — the most practical planner for deep-sky observing.
nextDarkNight.how_he: We scan 30 days ahead at ~01:00 and pick the dip in moon illumination (ephemeris, certain). Cloud is factored in only if the night falls within Open-Meteo's 3-day forecast horizon, otherwise null = 'too far to forecast, but moonless'.
nextDarkNight.caveat_he: The moon timing is certain (ephemeris); the cloud is forecast only for the short term. A combined estimate.

galacticCore.what_he: When the galactic core (Sagittarius, the dense and impressive part) is above ~12° and the sky is also dark — the photo/observing window.
galacticCore.how_he: Pure ephemeris: we convert RA 17.76h / Dec −28.9° to local elevation/azimuth (Astro.eqToHorizon) over the night; we mark the window where the core is ≥12° and the sun is also ≤−15°. We return the peak elevation, the direction, and the worst moon interference.
data_he: RA/Dec of the core (constant), sun and moon paths: Astro (ephemeris)
galacticCore.caveat_he: Accurate ephemeris. inSeason=false in winter (the core isn't above the horizon during darkness). Cloud/a low southern horizon are not included.

zodiacalLight.what_he: The interplanetary dust cone along the ecliptic ('false dawn/dusk') — a Bortle-3 sky phenomenon that Alex can actually see.
zodiacalLight.how_he: We measure the ecliptic's angle to the horizon at the moment of astronomical dusk/dawn (−18°): rise/run between two ecliptic points 10° apart. Steep (→90°) = the cone stands high = good; flat = swallowed in the glow. We also check for moon interference.
data_he: The sun's ecliptic longitude + the moon's path: Astro (ephemeris)
Quality threshold: angle ≥50° and a non-interfering moon
zodiacalLight.caveat_he: A classic ecliptic criterion (ephemeris). Not a guarantee — it requires a clean low horizon + his dark sky. A guidance estimate.

overnightPasses.title_he: What passed over the house while you slept
overnightPasses.what_he: Real satellite passes (ISS, etc.) that would have been naked-eye visible over the house in the last 14 hours.
overnightPasses.how_he: Real SGP4 propagation (Satellites) backward 14 h: a satellite is visible only if it's sunlit, the observer is in darkness (sun <−4°) and the elevation >10°. For each pass: peak elevation, direction and time.
overnightPasses.caveat_he: Accurate if the TLE is current; accuracy drops as the TLE ages. The time is the real now, not the scrubbing clock.

moonPhase.what_he: The illumination percentage, the name (new/full…) and the moon's apparent size — determines how much it 'washes out' faint stars.
moonPhase.how_he: Low-precision Meeus model: illumination = (1−cos(elongation angle from the sun))/2. The distance (km) from the ELP series gives an apparent diameter of ~0.49°–0.56° (including a 'supermoon').
moonPhase.caveat_he: Low-precision ephemeris — excellent for phase and illumination; not for precise eclipse calculations.

zodiacChart.what_he: Which sign and degree each body (sun/moon/planets) sits in — the ephemeris is real; the astrology is meant as poetry/for fun.
zodiacChart.how_he: From each body's geocentric ecliptic longitude (real) → a sign (30° each) and a degree within it. The rising horizon (ascendant) requires a birth time + place and is computed from the local sidereal time.
data_he: Ascendant: Astro.lstRad + the latitude (an estimate for the birthplace)
zodiacChart.caveat_he: The bodies' positions are astronomically real. The astrological interpretation is presented as poetry, not science. The ascendant is an estimate (the app's longitude as a proxy for the birthplace).

record_dliSum.what_he: The sum of the real light doses the corner has accumulated since the log began — building up day after day from real weather.
record_dliSum.how_he: Σ of the daily DLI from each recorded day in the range. Each day: that date's measured radiation (Open-Meteo) is fed through the corner's real shadowMask/POA (Derive.recordDay).
data_he: Daily measured radiation: RecordApi → Open-Meteo archive+forecast
record_dliSum.caveat_he: The weather is based on real measurements; the shading is computed from the geometry. The log accumulates only from the date it was opened — not infinite history.

record_zoneTotals.what_he: What the corner/plant has actually experienced so far: days, frost nights, total light (DLI), sun hours, GDD, chill hours, evaporation and rain.
record_zoneTotals.how_he: Accumulation of real day records (Derive.recordDay) over the range: dliSum, sunHoursSum, gddSum, chillSum, etcSum, rainSum, frostNights, and temperature extremes (tMinAbs/tMaxAbs). Measured weather × computed geometry.
data_he: Hourly measured weather: Open-Meteo (archive+forecast) via RecordApi
record_zoneTotals.caveat_he: Based on real measurements through the house geometry — not a physical sensor in the soil. Accumulated only from the start of the log; the archive covers up to ~5 days back (the rest from the forecast).

record_status.what_he: How many days have already been collected in the live log, from what date, and how much of the backfill is complete.
record_status.how_he: days = number of days in the local archive; pct = 100·completed days/planned days. Backfill runs in the background during idle time, up to a year back.
record_status.caveat_he: Based on real measurements (Open-Meteo) through the house geometry — not a sensor. 'gaps' indicates windows that haven't been fetched yet/successfully.

record_prediction.what_he: An estimate of where the seasonal total or fruit ripening is headed, from the rate measured in the log so far.
record_prediction.how_he: A simple linear extrapolation: we take the accumulation to date (e.g. accumulated GDD) and continue at the current rate, or cross-reference it against the plant's target (gdd_to_fruit / chill_hours_req) to estimate when it will be reached. The basis: past measurements; the continuation: the assumption that the rate persists.
record_prediction.caveat_he: A forecast, not a fact: a linear extrapolation from the rate so far — sensitive to weather variability and to seasonality. Low-to-medium confidence, growing as the log gets longer. Estimate.

cosmic_odometer.what_he: How many days/hours/solar orbits/full moons/Perseids you've experienced since you were born — the 'cosmic odometer'.
cosmic_odometer.how_he: Computed from the estimated birth date up to today: days=day difference, orbit=days·~107M km/day around the sun, full moons≈days/29.53, Perseids=number of years. Values frozen in resident_numbers.json as of 1.1.2026.
data_he: Astronomical constants (year length, lunar cycle, orbital circumference)
cosmic_odometer.caveat_he: Based on an estimated birth date (1.1.2000) — adjust it if needed. The values are frozen to the reference date; they only 'age' when the file is refreshed.

milestones_counter.what_he: How much time has passed since your life events: the move to Larkmont, moving into the house, the mortgage.
milestones_counter.how_he: A simple day difference between the event date (milestones.json / site.json) and today.
milestones_counter.caveat_he: Based on manually entered dates — as accurate as those are.

iNaturalist_sightings.what_he: Species (plants/animals) recently documented within a ~15 km radius of the house — real community data.
iNaturalist_sightings.how_he: A live call to the iNaturalist API around your coordinates, filtered to 'research-grade' observations that have a photo, sorted newest-first. By species/season on request.
data_he: Observations: iNaturalist API (lat/lng radius 15 km, research_grade)
iNaturalist_sightings.caveat_he: Based on real community observations — but it depends on observer coverage in the region (biases and sparsity are possible). Not a systematic survey.
